coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots and gold nanostructures on photothermal therapy for cancer treatment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Montazer, Leila
Mahani, Mohamad
Khakbaz, Faeze
Divsar, Faten
Yoosefian, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on the comparison of the photothermal (PT) properties of the new carbon quantum dots with two types of gold nanostructures including gold nanoparticles and gold nanorods.We prepared and characterized CQDs doped with copper from ascorbic acid precursor (Cu-aCQDs) with 3 nm diameter and citric acid precursor (Cu-cCQDs) with 5 nm diameter by hydrothermal method, Gold nanoparticles (GNP) were synthesized using sodium sulfide reduction and Gold nanorods (GNR) (aspect ratio of 5) by growth methods. UV–Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to analyze the optical and morphological properties of the nanoparticles. The fluorescence quantum yields of 2.14 % and 1.55 % were obtained for Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs respectively. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of 44 %, 66 %, 42 % and 57 % were obtained for Cu-aCQDs, Cu-cCQDs, GNPs and GNRs respectively. We also evaluated the toxicity and the photothermal therapy (PTT) performance of the nanoparticles on MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The ratio of the cell death (from photothermal agent) in the presence of LASER irradiation to its absence for Cu-aCQDs, Cu-cCQDs, GNPS and gold nanorods were 2.5, 5.3, 1.75 and 1.9 respectively. The CQDs had very low toxicity in the absence of LASER irradiation and only 15 % toxicity was obtained for 14.0 mg/mL of Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs, 350 μg/mL of GNPs and 87.5 of μg/mL gold nano rods. These results indicate that CQDs are less harmful than gold nanostructures in the absence of LASER irradiation and more effective than gold nanostructures when LASER irradiation is applied.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal treatment
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Gold nanorods
Photothermal conversion efficiency
MCF-7 cells

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of the quantum efficiency of fluorescence emission
Evaluation of the PT performance of nanoparticles
In vitro PTT of AuNPs and AuNRs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of Cu-CQDs prepared by two different precursors: citric acid (Cu-cCQDs – solid line) and ascorbic acid (Cu-aCQDs – dash line).
Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Cu-cCQDs and (B) Cu-aCQDs at different excitation wavelengths;
Size distribution of (A) Cu-cCQDs, and (B) Cu-aCQDs using dynamic light scattering method. (C) HRTEM of the Cu-cCQDs. (D) X-ray diffraction pattern of Cu-cCQDs.
FTIR absorption spectrum of (A) Cu-cCQDs, and (B) Cu-aCQDs.

(A) Absorption spectrum of the gold nanoparticles; (B) Size distribution of the gold nanoparticles by dynamic light scattering method; (C) Absorption spectrum of the colloidal gold nanorods; (D) Size distribution of the gold nanorods by dynamic light scattering method.

Temperature rise rate and PT stability diagram for 14 mg/mL Cu-CQDs with different carbon sources: A and C for Cu-cCQDs, B and D for Cu-aCQDs. Figures A and B were obtained under different LASER radiation intensity: a) 0.5 W/cm2 b) 1 W/cm2 c) 1.5 W/cm2 d) 2 W/cm2.
Regression of the natural logarithm of the driving force temperature versus time for (A) Cu-cCQDs and (B) Cu-aCQDs.

Temperature changes against time for (A) gold nanoparticles and (B) gold nanorods under the radiation intensity of a) 0.5 W/cm2 b) 1 W/cm2 c) 1.5 W/cm2 d) 2 W/cm2; Stability diagram of PT effect in (C) gold nanoparticles and (D) gold nanorods.
Regression of the natural logarithm of the driving force temperature versus time for (A) GNPs and (B) GNRs.

The survival percentage of MCF-7 breast cancer cells A: After treatment with different concentrations of Cu-cCQDs, LASER and both Cu-cCQDs and LASER, B: Temperature changes versus time due to LASER iradiation for PBS, water and different concentrations of Cu-cCQDs. C and D are same as A and B except for Cu-aCQDs in place of Cu-cCQDs. All the LASER powers are 2 W/cm2 for 5 min at 808 nm.

The survival percentage of MCF-7 breast cancer cells A: After treatment with different concentrations of gold nanoparticles, LASER and both gold nanoparticles and LASER, B: Temperature changes versus time due to LASER iradiation for PBS, water and different concentrations of gold nanoparticles. C and D are same as A and B except for gold nanorods in place of gold nanoparticles. All the LASER powers are 2 W/cm2 for 5 min at 808 nm.
Cytotoxic activity of two types Cu-CQDs with LASER irradiation.
Cytotoxic activity of AuNPs and AuNRs with LASER irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots and gold nanostructures on photothermal therapy for cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman 7631818356, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman 7631818356, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departments of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), PO BOX 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departments of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), PO BOX 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on the comparison of the photothermal (PT) properties of the new carbon quantum dots with two types of gold nanostructures including gold nanoparticles and gold nanorods.We prepared and characterized CQDs doped with copper from ascorbic acid precursor (Cu-aCQDs) with 3 nm diameter and citric acid precursor (Cu-cCQDs) with 5 nm diameter by hydrothermal method, Gold nanoparticles (GNP) were synthesized using sodium sulfide reduction and Gold nanorods (GNR) (aspect ratio of 5) by growth methods. UV–Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to analyze the optical and morphological properties of the nanoparticles. The fluorescence quantum yields of 2.14 % and 1.55 % were obtained for Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs respectively. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of 44 %, 66 %, 42 % and 57 % were obtained for Cu-aCQDs, Cu-cCQDs, GNPs and GNRs respectively. We also evaluated the toxicity and the photothermal therapy (PTT) performance of the nanoparticles on MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The ratio of the cell death (from photothermal agent) in the presence of LASER irradiation to its absence for Cu-aCQDs, Cu-cCQDs, GNPS and gold nanorods were 2.5, 5.3, 1.75 and 1.9 respectively. The CQDs had very low toxicity in the absence of LASER irradiation and only 15 % toxicity was obtained for 14.0 mg/mL of Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs, 350 μg/mL of GNPs and 87.5 of μg/mL gold nano rods. These results indicate that CQDs are less harmful than gold nanostructures in the absence of LASER irradiation and more effective than gold nanostructures when LASER irradiation is applied.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide, affecting millions of people every year. Despite the advances in conventional cancer therapies, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy, many patients still suffer from severe side effects, low efficacy, and high costs. Therefore, there is a need for alternative and innovative approaches to treat cancer more effectively and safely. One of these approaches is Photothermal therapy (PTT), a therapeutic modality that uses light-absorbing agents, such as nanoparticles, to selectively heat and destroy cancerous cells or other abnormal tissues while leaving healthy cells unharmed [1,2].Nanoparticles can absorb light at specific wavelengths and convert their energy into heat through a process called photothermal (PT) conversion. By choosing nanoparticles that absorb light in the near-infrared (NIR) region of the electromagnetic spectrum, which has a high tissue penetration depth and low absorption by biological molecules, PTT can achieve a high degree of selectivity and minimally invasiveness. However, PTT also faces some challenges, such as optimizing the targeting efficiency and clearance of the nanoparticles, improving the PT conversion efficiency and stability, and minimizing the off-target effects and toxicity [3–6]. One of the most commonly used nanoparticles for PTT is gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) which can be easily accumulated in tumor tissue by the enhanced permeability and retention effect of the tumor microenvironment [7]. Also, the high affinity of AuNPs to bind thiols, amines, and polymers makes them to be easily functionalized with a variety of targeting ligands, such as antibodies or peptides, which can enhance their specificity for tumor cells and tissues [8,9]. However, there are still challenges in the development of PTT using AuNPs, such as low PT conversion efficiency, limited light absorption in the NIR region, optimizing the targeting efficiency and clearance of the nanoparticles, improving the penetration depth of light into tissue, and minimizing off-target effects. Numerous attempts have been undertaken to create anisotropic gold nanostructures, including but not limited to nanorods, nanostars, and multipods [10,11]. Gold nanorods (AuNRs) have garnered significant interest among these structures, mainly because of their ability to demonstrate shape-dependent optical properties that can be adjusted as needed [12]. As a result, there is high demand for the efficient and dependable synthesis of AuNRs that can exhibit a wide range of longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (SPR) in the NIR region, which results in a high PT conversion efficiency and a low LASER power density requirement. However, AuNRs also face some challenges, such as the difficulty and variability of their synthesis, the instability and aggregation of their shape and size, and the cytotoxicity of their surfactants.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained notice for their PT properties, along with other nanomaterials [13]. CQDs with a diameter of less than 10 nm have unique optical and electronic properties that make them promising for PTT. CQDs can be designed as NIR absorbers, which have the ability to penetrate deeply into tissue [14]. One of the advantages of CQDs over other nanomaterials is their biocompatibility and low toxicity. CQDs are made of carbon, which is a naturally occurring element in the body, and they can be easily cleared from the body through renal excretion. In addition, CQDs can be easily functionalized with targeting ligands to enhance their selectivity for cancer cells [15]. Another advantage of CQDs is their low cost and ease of synthesis, which makes them attractive for large-scale production and potential clinical translation. Despite the promising potential of CQDs for PTT, some contests such as PT conversion efficiency, tumor targeting, and their stability and biocompatibility need to be improved. CQDs that are doped with various elements such as nitrogen, sulfur, gold, palladium, copper, iron oxide, and carbon nanotubes, have emerged as a promising material for PTT due to their unique PT properties [16,17]. For example, copper-doped CQDs (Cu-CQDs) with d–d transitions of copper ions strongly absorb near-infrared (NIR) irradiation and effectively generate heat [18–23].

The PT performance of nanomaterials is influenced by various factors such as size, shape, surface chemistry, and aggregation state. [24]. However, there is a lack of comprehensive and systematic comparison of these nanoparticles in terms of their PT properties, PTT efficacy, and toxicity. Moreover, there is a need for more standardized and reliable methods to evaluate and compare the PT performance and PTT efficiency of different nanoparticles, as well as to assess their biocompatibility and biosafety [25].

Based on our knowledge there are no comprehensive studies that compare the PT properties of Cu-CQDs with gold nanostructures. In this work, two sizes Cu-CQDs from different precursors as well as gold nanostructures with different shapes were synthesized and their PT efficiency was investigated regarding different parameters such as concentration, LASER excitation intensity, and exposure time. Also the toxicity and photothermal therapeutic efficiencies of these nanostructures were investigated on MCF-7 cells.

All chemical reagents were used without any treatment. Citric acid, disodium sulfide, copper (II) chloride, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), silver nitrate, sodium borohydride and glutathione were purchased from Merck company (Merck, Germany). Quinine homosulfate, ascorbic acid, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), DMSO, and dithiothreitol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company (Missouri, United States). Double distilled water was used throughout experiments.

Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DEMD) and trypsin-ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid X 10 were from Biosera company. Fetal bovine serum was prepared by Gibco company (Gibco, USA). MCF-7 cell line breast cancer cells were prepared from the cell bank of the Pasteur Institute of Iran (Tehran, Iran).

Spectrofluorimeter ((Varian, Cary Eclipse, Australia) and an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 100, Australia) were used for recording the absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN200 KV, UK) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D-8 Advance, Germany) were used for characterization of the synthesized nanoparticles. FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, USA) was applied to structural analysis and identification of the functional groups on the surface of the quantum dots. The zeta sizer (Malvern, UK) was used for characterization of the synthesized nanoparticles in terms of hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity. 808 nm diode LASERs (Mehr optics, HYPERTHERMIA LASER - HTL-V-808, Iran) was used for LASER irradiation. Cell viability was studied by a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, ELX 808, Germany).

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize Cu-CQDs using two different precursors. Briefly, 0.26 g of ascorbic acid and 0.26 g of copper (II) chloride were added to 15 mL of distilled water and stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 2 min. The solution was transferred to autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the resulting collide was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min and stored at room temperature. Alternatively, for synthesis of Cu-CQDs with citric acid precursor, 0.30 g of citric acid were mixed with 0.26 g of copper (II) chloride in 15 mL of distilled water. The solution was stirred until clear solution is produced and then transferred to autoclave and heated same as before [26].

Gold nanoparticles were synthesized using the method of reduction by sodium sulfide. For the synthesis of gold nanoparticles, first, 10 mL of 1 M chloroauric acid solution was diluted to 20 mL with distilled water. Then, 50 µL of 0.1 M ice-cold disodium sulfide solution (6 °C) was added to the solution. Stired vigorously for 30 s. The resulting pale pink colloid was stored at 4 °C until use.

The growth method was employed to synthesize gold nanorods without the use of a seed solution. The synthesis process involved mixing 5 mL of a 1 mM chloroauric acid solution with an equal volume of 0.2 M cetyltrimethylammonium bromide. 270 µL of a 4 mM silver nitrate solution were added and vortexed for 30 s, followed by the addition of 8 µL of 37 % hydrochloric acid. Then 70 µL of the 87.7 mM ascorbic acid solution were added while keeping the pH level at about 1. After vortexing for 30 s, the solution became completely colorless. 15 µL of 0.01 M sodium borohydride were added to facilitate the formation of nanorods. Then the solution was kept still at room temperature for 24 h [27]. In this synthesis the presence of surfactants and Ag causes a disruption in the symmetry of gold nanorods by selectively depositing on the [110] facets. This selective deposition inhibits growth in other directions and promotes growth parallel to the [001] plane, ultimately resulting in the formation of anisotropic gold nanorods [27]. The synthesized nanorods were kept at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the quantum efficiency of fluorescence emission
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the quantum yield (QY) of the Cu-CQDs, a comparative method [28] was employed. A reference solution of quinine sulfate (QY of 54 % at 360 nm) was dissolved in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution. The Cu-CQDs colloids, and quinine hemi sulfate solutions with various concentrations were prepared and their photoluminescence and absorption spectra were recorded. Their fluorescence emissions were regressed versus their absorbance’s. In the absence of scattering and secondary absorption effects, the fluorescence intensity and absorption increases linearly with concentration.

MCF-7 cells were grown in 25 cm2 culture flasks containing complete medium (RPMI 1640 medium, 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum, and 1 % (v/v) penicillin–streptomycin) at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 until they reached at least 70 % confluency. Next, the standard MTT assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of Cu-CQDs and gold nanostructures on MCF-7 cells. A quantity of 1 × 104 cells/well of MCF-7 cells was used to seed in 96-well plates, which were then incubated overnight at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. Next, the cells were subjected to different concentrations of Cu-CQDs (0.06, 0.12, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mM). The cells were incubated with MTT solution at 37 °C for 4 h. Following the removal of MTT and the addition of 100 μL of DMSO to the cells, the absorbance value was recorded at wavelength 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the PT performance of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the photothermal properties of the synthesized colloidal nanoparticles, three milliliters of them (see section 3.3 and 3.4 for concentrations), was irradiated in a 3.5 × 1 × 1 cm cell using a 808 nm diode LASER with a beam cross section of 1 cm2 at a distance of 1 cm from the cell. The temperature was recorded at different time intervals ((see section 3.3 and 3.4) and different LASER intensities (0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 W/cm2) was placed and the resulting temperature was recorded.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in a 96-well microplate. Then, the experiment was followed by the addition of Cu-CQDs at varying concentrations (ranging from 0.0625 to 1 mM) and subjected to a 24-hour incubation period. Next, the cells were treated with a LASER (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 5 min), and after 24 h, cytotoxicity was evaluated using the MTT assay, as previously reported [28]. The same procedure was carried out using AuNPs with concentrations ranging from 15.6 to 250 mM and AuNRs with concentrations ranging from 3.9 to 62.5 mM.

Copper doping plays a critical role in CQDs exhibiting a new class of NIR‐emitting fluorophores [22]. However, carbon sources used in the synthesis of CQDs have a significant impact on their properties, including size, morphology, functional groups on the surface, and fluorescence properties. The optical properties of Cu-CQDs were explored using UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectroscopy.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of two types of Cu-CQD prepared by citric acid (Cu-cCQD), and ascorbic acid (Cu-aCQD) as carbon sources, respectively. Cu-cCQDs with a sp2 graphitic carbon core and polar oxygen-containing functional groups show the peaks related to
π →
π
∗
transition of graphitic structure and
n →
π
∗
transition of C=O and aromatic sp2 domains in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1 – solid line) [29].

The absorbance peak of Cu-aCQD shows also peaks related to
π →
π
∗
transition of C=C and n → π* transition of C=O, demonstrating the structural functional group alteration in the ascorbic acid compare to citric acid prepared Cu-CQDs (Fig. 1 – dash line).
Both synthesized Cu-CQDs show absorbance at 810 nm, indicating the copper is doped in the CQDs.

Fig. 2 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Cu-CQDs. As it can be observed, for Cu-cCQDs the highest excitation occurs at approximately 350 nm (Fig. 2A). When Cu-cCQDs are excited by different wavelengths at 340, 350, 360, and 370, the corresponding PL emission peaks are located at 448, 450, 430, and 420 nm, respectively. By increasing the excitation wavelength to more than 350 nm, the emission wavelength was blue-shifted with a gradual lowering of PL intensity. Cu-aCQDs’ fluorescence emission spectra are presented in Fig. 2B, showcasing a redshift of roughly 30 nm in the emission peak as the excitation wavelength ranges from 370 to 410 nm. The photoluminescence emission of Cu-CQDs appears to be influenced by the surface state of the particles, which has an impact on their band structure. One possible explanation for this behavior is the presence of various surface emissive trap sites, which may arise from differences in particle size, complex Cu doping, or other factors [30]. This can also be attributed to the existence of carbon resources from different organic compounds in the precursor of CQDs. [31]. Fig. 2 shows that Cu-aCQDs display the highest emission at 450 nm, which falls within the visible blue light range, whereas Cu-cCQDs exhibit a shift to approximately 420 nm. Compare to the ascorbic acid, the use of citric acid precursor results in a blue shift of the PL of Cu-CQDs. Thus, the production of Cu-CQDs using different precursors is affected by the synergistic effects of the sp2-conjugated domain, degree of graphitization, and surface functional group contents [32].

Fig. 3 shows a monodispersed-sized distribution of the synthesized Cu-CQDs, with hydrodynamic diameters of approximately 5 nm and 3 nm for citric acid, and ascorbic acid, respectively, obtained by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The synthesized Cu-cCQDs also characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 3C shows their HRTEM image. It is clear that they have a graphitic, spherical structure with a layer spacing of about 0.2 nm.

The X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon quantum dots is also shown in the Fig. 3D. Due to the graphitic structure of the carbon quantum dots and few number of layers, their X-ray diffraction pattern shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ about 26° from (200) plane that is inconsistent with reported patterns in literature.

The surface composition of two types of Cu-CQDs is depicted in Fig. 4 through the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. The FT-IR spectrum of Cu-cCQDs is represented in Fig. 4A. The broadband in the region of 3100–3700 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibrations of O−H in hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups. The peak around 2069.43 cm−1 could be ascribed as the stretching vibrations of the C-H bond peak that matched our previous FT-IR of CQDs [33]. The peak at 1639.67 and 1216 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C-O in CQDs, respectively. The peak in the region of 430–700 cm-1 is associated with the existence of copper in the CQDs composition. The FTIR of Cu-aCQDs (Fig. 4B) shows the peaks around the 3458, 2122, 1638, and 1219 cm−1 that are associated with the stretching vibrations of O−H, C=C or C−H, C=O, and C-O, respectively. In addition, the other peak around the 435–677 cm−1 can be attributed to Cu–O bond stretching that matched with the previous studies [34,35]. Following the doping procedure, there were noticeable shifts and alterations in the peaks associated with C=O, C-H, and C-O in the CQDs. These changes suggest that the doping process was effective. The primary coordination of copper was observed with the –COOH groups [34]. The alterations in the intensity and position of the C=O (1638 cm−1) and C-O (1092 cm−1) peaks in the CQDs were likely caused by the intraction of electrons with oxygen, as described in the earlier CQDs FT-IR analysis [35].

Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the aqueous solution of Cu-CQDs was subjected to measurement of its fluorescent quantum yield (QY), which was found to be 1.55 % and 2.14 % for Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs respectively. Williams et al. [36] have reported that the optical properties and QY of both CQDs and Cu-CQDs are linked to factors such as particle size, modification, and heteroatom doping. This suggests that these materials have potential for use in a wide range of applications [37].

To confirm the synthesis of gold nanostructures, it is a common practice to obtain UV–visible spectra. Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) is a unique optical characteristic displayed by AuNPs. The position and intensity of the LSPR band are determined by the surface morphology and size of the gold particles. An increase in particle size leads to a shift of the plasmon peak towards higher wavelengths [38]. The LSPR band of the synthesized gold nanoparticles appeared in the range of 500-600 nm (525 nm peak) in their absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 A) Fig. 5B displays the size distribution of chemically synthesized AuNPs, as determined by DLS measurements. It can be observedthat the gold nanoparticles range from 8 to 35 nm in size, with an average size of 15 nm.

Nanorods are formed based on the structure-directing elongation of seeds. The anisotropic shape of AuNRs induces a splitting of the SPR into transverse and longitudinal modes. Characteristically two plasmon resonances of AuNRs are observed in Fig. 5C. The initial peak, located at approximately 520 nm, corresponds to the surface plasmon resonance in a transverse direction similar to that of nanospheres. The second peak located in the wavelength interval of 650–850 nm and the result of the surface plasmon resonance that occurs at longitudinal direction. However, he aspect ratio (length/width) of gold nanorods has a significant impact on their absorption spectrum. As the aspect ratio of the nanorod increases, the SPR absorption peak of the longitudinal band experiences a significant redshift [39]. In Fig. 5C, the formation of AuNRs is characterized by the emergence of both transverse and longitudinal plasmon resonances. In addition, the existence of two distinct peaks indicates that the sample was uniform. The same phenomenon was also observed in previous work [12]. Gold nanorods with specific aspect ratios display two peaks in their size distribution diagram, with one peak having an average hydrodynamic diameter of 2–4 nm and the other peak ranging from 11 to 16 nm (Fig. 5D). The presence of the first peak is ascribed to seed AuNPs that were unable to grow due to the inadequacy of gold in the growth solution, while the second peak is indicative of fully-formed AuNRs.

An important attribute of Cu-CQDs is their PT effect. To evaluate the PT properties, a 14 mg/mL colloidal solution of Cu-CQDs was irradiated using 808 nm LASER irradiation and the temperature rise profile was studied with 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 W/cm2 intensities during 10 min. The temperature was monitored with a thermal imaging camera. Fig. 6 A and 6B show the behavior of 14 mg/mL Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs after 10 min’ irradiation using 0.5–2 W/cm2 LASER respectively. During the first 4 min of irradiation, we have the highest rate of temperature increase, which is about 6 and 5 degrees Celsius per minute for Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs respectively. After 10 min irradiation with 2 W/cm2 LASER, the maximum temperatures of 55.3 and 62.0 °C were obtained for Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs respectively. Considering that cancer cells are stable at a maximum temperature of 46 °C, these carbon quantum dots can be used as a cancer nanotherapeutic agent. Based on the difference in the results obtained, it can be said that the type of carbon source directly alters the physical and chemical properties of CQDs including surface chemistry and degree of their crystallinity which influence their PT efficiency. Carbon quantum dots synthesized using citric acid as the carbon source are more efficient in PT performance compared to those synthesized using ascorbic acid as the carbon source.

The PT conversion efficiency (η) of CuCQDs (with different carbon sources) was calculated [40]. The Cu-CQDs colloid (14 mg/mL) was irradiated using LASER (808 nm, 1.2 W) until the maximum temperature was reached, and after that, the system was allowed to cool down by stopping the LASER. The time constant of the system (
τ s
) is the
- 1
slope
of the regression of the
l n ( θ )
(where
θ =
Δ T
Δ T
maximum
) versus time in the cooling cycle (see Fig. 7 A and 7B for Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs respectively). The time constants can be used for calculating the product of the heat transfer coefficient and surface area (
hs
) using equation (1).
(1)
h s =
∑
m i
C
p . i
τ s
where the
m i
is the mass of the ith system components and
c p
is its specific heat capacity. Finally, the LASER heat conversion efficiency ( η ) were calculated using equation (2).
(2)
η =
hs
Δ T
maximum
-
P
water + c e l l
P
LASER
( 1 -
10
-
A
CQD
)

The
P
water + c e l l
(generated heat power by water and cell) was calculated independently using the maximum temperature that was obtained after irradiating the cell with water and without CQDs by the same power of the LASER. A PT conversion efficiency of 44 % and 66 % was calculated for the Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs respectively. These η values indicating their effectiveness as PT agents in tumor treatments. This value was found to be extremely desirable compared to other nanoparticles studied for PT therapy.

Furthermore, PT stability is a crucial consideration for photothernal agents. In order to evaluate this characteristic, a collide of Cu-CQDs (14 mg/mL) was subjected to five heat-cool cycles by irradiating it with a LASER of relatively high-power intensity (2 W/cm2). Fig. 6C and 6D demonstrate that the temperature of Cu-CQDs increased during the “on” phase of irradiation and decreased during the “off” phase for each cycle. This indicates that the Cu-CQDs possess exceptional PT stability. However, for Cu-cCQDs, during the subsequent cycles, the temperature difference was observed to be approximately 5 °C higher than in the first cycle. Each new heating cycle started before the solution had sufficient time to cool back down to room temperature, resulting in this occurrence [41].

Modifying the morphology of gold can enhance its PT properties, thereby making PT therapy more effective. Asymmetrical structures can be observed in elongated, anisotropic nanoparticles such as rod-shaped particles. When electron oscillation is excited along the shorter axis of certain structures, they create a plasmon band at a wavelength comparable to that of Au nanospheres. This band is known as the transverse band. On the other hand, exciting electron oscillation along the longer axis generates a much stronger plasmon band in the longer wavelength region, referred to as the longitudinal band. The generation of heat caused by plasmonic oscillation in the NIR region from the longitudinal band is the foundation for PTT applications using plasmonic nanoparticles. Gold nanostructures capture energy when exposed to a LASER with a wavelength of 808 nm. This energy is then transferred to the surrounding medium through plasmonic excitation, resulting in the conversion of light into heat. D'Acunto suggests that the conversion of light to heat in AuNPs occurs within a picosecond, and the resulting energy dissipation into the surrounding medium causes a rise in temperature. This energy can be harnessed for thermotherapy, allowing for the targeted elimination of specific body tissues. [42]. In addition, it is essential to regulate this energy in order to eliminate cancerous cells while also preserving healthy tissue, as malignant cells necessitate merely half the amount of energy required by healthy cells [43]. The amount of energy transferred is contingent on the shape, size, and composition of the physiological medium. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the application of LASER power densities on 350.0
μ g / m L
of AuNPs colloids resulted in an increase in temperature, with the maximum temperature reaching nearly 50 °C for a duration of 600 s at 2.0 W/cm2 power density. This temperature is lower than that of Cu-cCQDs. During the first 4 min, we have the highest rate of temperature increase, which is about 3 degrees Celsius per minute and half of the value reported in the previous section for Cu-cCQDs. The plasmonic oscillation in a rod-shaped colloid occurs in two directions due to the anisotropic feature of the particles, and its intensity increases as the electromagnetic radiation frequency shifts from the visible to the near-infrared (NIR) region. This phenomenon causes that the lower concentration of rod-form particles (87.5
μ g / m L
) to attain a higher temperature compared to the spheres, as depicted in Fig. 8B. As it is clear in this figure, the application of LASER power densities on 87.5
μ g / m L
of AuNRs colloids resulted in an increase in temperature, with the maximum temperature reaching nearly 52 °C for a duration of 600 s at 2.0 W/cm2 power density. This temperature is also lower than that of Cu-cCQDs and also the rate of temperature rise during the first 4 min was about 4.5 degrees Celsius per minute and lower than the value reported in the previous section for Cu-cCQDs. Fig. 8A and 8B show that for all LASER powers the maximum attainable temperature for 87.5
μ g / m L
gold nanorods are higher than the 350.0
μ g / m L
gold nanospheres. The strong PT conversion properties of gold nanorods paved the way for PTT.

Fig. 8C demonstrates that the temperature of Au NPs rose when irradiation was initiated, and fell when it was ceased for each ON/OFF cycle, revealing the exceptional photothermal stability. The temperature curve in Fig. 8D also shows no obvious change, indicating the high PT stability of Au-NRs. However, in the case of the gold nanorods, During the subsequent cycles, the ΔT was approximately 5 °C higher than that of the first cycle due to incomplete cooling of the solution to room temperature before the start of each new heating cycle [41]. Fig. 9 A and 9B show the changing of the temperature driving force during the OFF-cycle of the LASER. The calculated time constants were used for calculation of photothermal conversion efficiency (same as that calculated for CQDs). The calculated conversion efficiencies of AuNPs and AuNRs were obtained 42 % and 57 %, respectively.

The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) proliferation assay was employed to assess the cytotoxicity of Cu-CQDs on MCF-7 cells. Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs were administered to cells at various concentrations (0.8, 1.7, 3.5, 7.1 and 14.0 mg/mL) for 12 h. Fig. 10 A-D summarizes the effect of different concentrations of Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs on MCF-7 cancer cells. In this figure, the maximum achievable temperature of water, PBS solution and different concentrations (0.8, 1.7, 3.5, 7.1 and 14.0 mg/mL) of Cu-cCQDs and Cu-aCQDs are shown after 5 min of 2 W/cm2 LASER irradiation. As it is clear in the figures, both types of Cu-CQDs used, do not show significant toxicity even at the highest concentration and also LASER radiation alone does not kill cells, and 90 % of cells survived after 5 min of LASER irradiation (Fig. 10A and C). But LASER irradiation when the cells treated with 14.0 mg/mL Cu-cCQDs have led to 80 % of cell death. The effect of the Cu-aCQDs has been much less and the LASER irradiation of the cells that treated with 14.0 mg/mL Cu-aCQDs have led to the death of 40 % of the cells, which is definitely related to their lower photothermal efficiency and lower maximum temperature that can be reached by them. The ratio of the cell death (from photothermal agent) in the presence of LASER irradiation to its absence for 14.0 mg/mL Cu-aCQDs and Cu-cCQDs were 2.5 and 5.3 respectively. It is clear that the Cu-cCQD are more effective than Cu-aCQDs in the presence of LASER irradiation. Although higher temperature has led to the killing of more cancer cells, it should be noted that that PT therapy (PTT) at high temperatures (above 50 °C) typically leads to necrosis, while temperatures below 50 °C usually result in cell apoptosis [44]. In general, temperatures below 50 °C produce less cell necrosis and minimize the risk of immunological reactions, making them more suitable for PTT applications that require protection of the surrounding environment [45]. Therefore, a concentration of 14.0 mg/mL Cu-CQDs was chosen for PT therapy.
According to the IC50 values (after 24 h treatment), the Cu-cCQDs were more cytotoxic to MCF-7 cells than Cu-aCQDs (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PTT of AuNPs and AuNRs
ce:section.ce:para: The PT measurement demonstrates the Au nanostructures have the potential as an effective PTT agent. We first investigate the biocompatibility of Au nanostructures by using cell counting MTT assay to evaluate their cytotoxicity against the MCF-7 (human breast cancer) cells. Cells were treated with different concentrations of AuNPs (71.87, 43.75, 87.5, 175 and 350 µg/mL) for 24 h. According to the results depicted in Fig. 11 A, the estimated cellular viability remains above 90 % at a mass concentration up to 350 µg/mL after 24 h, thereby indicating excellent biocompatibility of the AuNPs. When the cells exposed to 808 nm LASER for 5 min, more than 90 % of MCF-7 cells were found viable, but LASER radiation when the cells treated with 350 µg/mL AuNPs, have led to the death of 30 % of cells, implying the promising phototherapeutic effect of AuNPs in MCF-7 cells.

As can be seen, the percentage of cell survival in low concentrations of gold nanoparticles in the presence of LASER radiation has no significant difference from that of LASER radiation alone, but with the increase in the concentration of nanoparticles, this difference becomes significant. As can be seen in Fig. 11B, water and PBS solution do not show a significant change in temperature after LASER irradiation of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min, but in the presence of gold nanoparticles, this difference is significant, and the increase in the concentration of gold nanoparticles greatly increases the slope of the temperature raise indicating that Au NPs have good PT properties.

In addition, Fig. 11C shows the cell viability were the MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of AuNRs (5.7, 11.0, 21.9, 43.7 and 87.5 µg/mL) for 12 h. As displayed in the figure, in the absence of LASER irradiation, AuNRs demonstrate minimal cytotoxicity, and cell viability is slightly dependent on the dose of the nanorods. MCF-7 cells treated with AuNRs at a concentration of 87.5 µg/mL exhibit a high cell viability rate of 90.0 %. The results show that AuNRs do not appear to be significantly cytotoxic. In contrast, cell viability decreases significantly with increasing concentration of AuNRs under NIR LASER irradiation (808 nm, 300 s, 2 W/cm2). At a concentration of 87.5 µg/mL, the cell viability of AuNRs drops to 60 %, indicating that these nanorods can effectively eliminate tumor cells when exposed to NIR irradiation.

The PT performance of AuNRs at varying concentrations (5.7–87.5 µg/mL) was explored by subjecting them to an 808 nm LASER at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min and monitoring the temperature using an infrared thermometer. As illustrated in Fig. 11D, a significant difference is observed between the control samples (distilled water and PBS) and different concentrations of AuNRs, suggesting that the synthesized AuNRs under optimized conditions can efficiently convert LASER irradiation into heat and serve as an effective PT agent.

The ratio of the cell death (from photothermal agent) in the presence of LASER irradiation to its absence for GNPs (350 μg/mL) and gold nanorods (87.5 μg/mL) were 1.75 and 1.9 respectively. It is clear that the gold Nano rods are more effective than GNPs in the presence of LASER irradiation.

Against MCF-7 cells, the inhibitory concentration (IC50) value was 637.0 µg/mL Au for nanospheres, and 110.5 µg/mL Au for nanorods (Table 2 ). Thus, nanorods were more cytotoxic than nanospheres against MCF-7 cells. It can be also attributed to the higher internalization and PT effect of AuNRs under 808 NIR LASER exposure [46].

In summary, this study investigated the photothermal (PT) properties of copper-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized with two precursors and two gold nanostructures. The Cu-CQDs synthesized using CuCl2 and citric acid or ascorbic acid carbon source through the hydrothermal technique showed high efficiency of energy conversion, water solubility, fluorescence emission, and low toxicity towards living cells. Gold nanorods at lower concentrations exhibited higher PT efficiency than gold nanoparticles, attributable to the absorption peak of the gold nanorods in the infrared region. MCF-7 cell cytotoxicity investigations after LASER irradiation indicated significantly higher toxicity in AuNRs than in AuNPs, and Cu-cCQDs showed higher toxicity than Cu-aCQDs. Fluorescence property of Cu-CQDs in addition to thermal effect when treated with NIR LASER, making them a promising candidate for highly efficient imaging agent for PTT and cancer therapy. The CQDs also have advantages such as cost-effective synthesis process, short processing time, absorbance peak at NIR region (810 nm), small size of less than 5 nm, high photostability and great biocompatibility over gold nanostructures. As a result, the green synthesis of Cu-CQDs has the potential to be employed as a highly efficient imaging agent for PTT and cancer therapy in future.

We the undersigned declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us. We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process. He is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs.

Leila Montazer: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Mohamad Mahani: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Faeze Khakbaz: Writing – original draft. Faten Divsar: Writing – original draft. Mehdi Yoosefian: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
